topic: Eclipse magnetic ensemble (39 eclipses) — post-ISS-1470/1471/1472 re-attack
change_summary: Decider self-applied ISS-1470 (coverage term added to detail_verdict_text P2), ISS-1471 (invoke→cite in triple-count sentence), ISS-1472 (numerical qualifier added to Finding). Fresh adversarial attack confirms all three patches landed cleanly; surfaces one carry-forward moderate (scope leak, c11 H1), two new minors introduced/spread by the ISS-1472 patch, and three stale open issues (ISS-1441/1442/1443) not in scope of this push.
our_argument_summary: WIN-068 counts the Kim & Chang (2018) 39-eclipse (1991–2016) INTERMAGNET ensemble as a dome confirmation. We argue: (a) the signal is explained by the Chapman-Sq ionospheric mechanism without any dome parameters; (b) the 1991–2016 ensemble attaches no dome-derived numerical prediction — FSF is only invoked in the 2026 forward projection; (c) FSF = H(r)/r is a legitimate dome-geometry derivation (Pearson r=0.9999 between the dome's own formula and its published FSF values), but this is dome-vs-dome consistency, not dome-vs-observation, and it is out of scope for WIN-068's 1991–2016 ensemble; (d) WIN-010/025/068 triple-count the same ionospheric mechanism at three aggregation levels; (e) the dome adopted a published dataset 8 years post-publication with no pre-registration, failing the post-hoc test under the dome author's own timestamping definition.

relevant_files: raw-text/monitor.py (v50.6 snapshot — WIN-068 post-dates this snapshot); dome ascribes WIN-068 to the Kim & Chang (2018) INTERMAGNET dataset, not to its own monitor.py automation
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: No live calculation. WIN-068 cites Kim & Chang (2018) observational results. The FSF scaling formula (delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF, where FSF = H(r)/r normalized to Boulder) is invoked only for the 2026 forward projection (the WIN-010/025 cluster), not for the 1991–2016 ensemble.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: Retrospective literature citation, not a predicted value. Nothing in monitor.py validates WIN-068.
data_sources_used: Kim & Chang (2018), Advances in Space Research 61:2040–2054 (INTERMAGNET data)
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: None for the 1991–2016 ensemble — because no prediction is made.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None. The 'monitoring: none' code_analysis tag is correct.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: WIN-068 is a retrospective citation of Kim & Chang (2018); there is no monitor.py domain that fetches eclipse-ensemble data or compares it to a dome-predicted value. 'monitoring: none' is correct.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: The Chapman-Sq ionospheric mechanism has been standard physics since the early 20th century; the dome renames it 'firmament-plate dielectric boundary shading' but inherits the same predicted pattern without adding numerical prediction for the 1991–2016 ensemble.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Kim & Chang (2018) published 8 years before the dome incorporated this dataset (V51.0, early 2026). Under the dome author's own timestamping definition, no pre-registration of the expected value in a venue independent of the author exists. The 39-eclipse ensemble is a retrospective literature citation.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: For the 1991–2016 ensemble counted here: FSF is not invoked; no dome-derived numerical prediction is attached. (Separate from WIN-010's 2026 projection, where FSF = H(r)/r IS a legitimate dome-geometry derivation — but that scoping lives in WIN-010, not WIN-068.) Tag correctly remains False for WIN-068.

holes_found.description: Scope leak in detail_verdict_text P2 ("Credit where it is due on the scaling itself: the dome's 2026 eclipse formula (delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF) uses a field-strength factor that genuinely equals H(r)/r normalized to Boulder..."). This paragraph plus the parallel paragraph in detail_evidence P4 ("For completeness: the dome's FSF scaling factor (relevant to the WIN-010 2026 forward projection) is a legitimate geometric derivation...") spend substantial prose validating FSF AS DOME GEOMETRY, inside a WIN whose own scoping (P3/P5/Finding) explicitly says FSF does not enter the 1991–2016 ensemble. A dome defender can grab the verdict-text concession and run with it: 'the review concedes in WIN-068 that FSF is real dome geometry — Pearson r=0.9999 — so stop calling it empirical fitting.' The retraction is correct on the merits, but putting it inside WIN-068's verdict gives the defender a quote-mine that reads as a concession to THIS WIN rather than a cross-reference to WIN-010. This is the same c11 H1 scope leak in restructured form — the 'Even granting...' framing is gone, but the FSF-validation prose block remains inside WIN-068. (Pre-write staleness check confirms this text is live in the current wins.json — not superseded by any parallel patch.)
holes_found.recommendation: Compress P2 of detail_verdict_text and P4 of detail_evidence to a single cross-reference sentence: 'The dome's FSF = H(r)/r derivation (Pearson r=0.9999 between the dome's formula and its nine published FSF values) is a legitimate piece of dome geometry — that concession is germane to the WIN-010/025 2026 forward projection and is detailed there. For the 1991–2016 ensemble counted by WIN-068, FSF is not invoked.' Move the full FSF-validation prose to WIN-010's verdict (if it is not already there). This keeps WIN-068's scope clean and denies the defender a standalone FSF concession quote inside WIN-068.

holes_found.description: ISS-1472 patch spread the Chapman mis-specialization to the Finding. The revised Finding now opens 'Non-discriminating: Chapman (1933) ionospheric mechanism + Kim & Chang (2018) predict the 39-eclipse ensemble signal magnitude and geometry.' Chapman (1933, Terr. Mag. Atmos. Electr. 38:3) is about lunar geomagnetic tidal variations, not solar-eclipse ionospheric Sq. The canonical Sq-theory paper is Chapman (1919) Phil. Trans. A 218:1–118; eclipse-specific Sq application came later (Rastogi 1962, Malin 1973, culminating in modern treatments such as Yamazaki & Maute 2021). The ISS-1472 patch (narrowing 'dome-derived prediction' to 'dome-derived numerical prediction') was correct and additive, but it copied forward the same Chapman attribution already flagged in c11 H5 — so the Finding now inherits the defender wedge rather than quarantining it in the detail blocks.
holes_found.recommendation: Rewrite Finding opener as: 'Non-discriminating: the Chapman–Sq ionospheric framework (Chapman 1919, with eclipse-specific application in Rastogi 1962 and modern conductivity treatment in Yamazaki & Maute 2021) together with the Kim & Chang (2018) ensemble predicts the 39-eclipse signal magnitude and geometry.' Applies the same fix to detail_evidence ¶3 and detail_verdict_text ¶1. Single coordinated patch — attribution consistency across three fields.

holes_found.description: tldr_evidence mis-attributes the ensemble to the dome: 'The dome's ensemble of 39 eclipses spanning 1991–2016 (Kim & Chang 2018) confirms this ionospheric mechanism.' The 39-eclipse ensemble is Kim & Chang's, not the dome's — the dome cites it. Reading this in isolation a defender will say 'we never claimed the ensemble is ours, strawman.' The expanded detail_evidence P4 gets this right ('retrospective citation of the Kim & Chang (2018) 1991–2016 dataset'); the TLDR is the outlier. TLDR factual errors are major by the review rubric when they mislead, but here the imprecision does not change the TLDR's substantive conclusion ('confirms this ionospheric mechanism; does not require or validate dome cavity dynamics') — it is misattribution, not misdirection. Downgrading to minor.
holes_found.recommendation: Rewrite tldr_evidence as: 'Eclipse geomagnetic perturbations have been understood since the Chapman–Sq framework (1919 onward) — the moon's shadow reduces ionospheric conductivity and suppresses Sq currents. The dome claims WIN-068 by citing the Kim & Chang (2018) 39-eclipse 1991–2016 INTERMAGNET ensemble, which confirms this ionospheric mechanism without requiring or validating dome cavity dynamics.' Folds in the Chapman-mis-specialization fix from hole #2.

holes_found.description: Cross-WIN citation-format inconsistency: WIN-068 uses 'Chapman (1933, Terrestrial Magnetism and Atmospheric Electricity 38:3)' (ambiguous 38:3 — vol:page versus vol:issue — this is ISS-1441). WIN-010 uses 'Chapman (1933, Terr. Mag. Atmos. Electr.)' with no suffix. WIN-025 uses 'Chapman (1933)' with no journal info at all. Three different citation formats for the same paper across the WIN-010/025/068 cluster. A careful reader (or a defender) will notice. ISS-1441 has been open since c10 and was not in the ISS-1470/1471/1472 patch scope — still unresolved.
holes_found.recommendation: Coordinated patch across the WIN-010/025/068 cluster: settle on a single canonical Chapman citation format and use it consistently. If the intended reference is actually the Chapman–Sq foundational work, use 'Chapman (1919) Phil. Trans. A 218:1–118' everywhere (and add Kim & Chang (2018) for the modern ensemble). Addresses ISS-1441 and partially overlaps with hole #2.

holes_found.description: ISS-1442 still unresolved: the 'Steelman Evidence Ladder places eclipse geomagnetic evidence at Tier 1' sentence is verbatim-duplicated between detail_evidence ¶4 and detail_verdict_text ¶4. A readthrough of the expanded panel reads the exact same sentence twice within ~500 characters. Not in the ISS-1470/1471/1472 scope.
holes_found.recommendation: Keep the verdict-side occurrence (higher rhetorical weight), replace the evidence-side occurrence with a brief cross-reference: 'See the verdict for the dome's own Tier-1 Steelman admission, which concedes this WIN alone cannot distinguish cavity dynamics from standard ionospheric physics.'

holes_found.description: ISS-1443 still unresolved: detail_evidence ¶2 contains stale terminology: 'the refraction fix leaves physical sun altitude indeterminate — undermining the geometric shadow it claims for all 39 eclipses.' The phrase 'the refraction fix' predates the EXP-222/HNOTE-REFRACT-RESTRUCTURE-001 renaming to 'Coordinate Collapse' in Section 1.8. Other WIN verdicts in the cluster already use the new terminology; WIN-068 evidence lags behind.
holes_found.recommendation: Rephrase as 'the n(r) refractive contamination (Section 1.8 Coordinate Collapse) leaves physical sun altitude indeterminate' — consistent with the cluster's post-restructure vocabulary.

holes_found.description: verdict_history shows two verdict flips in a single day (2026-04-12 Std→Self-Contradicted via EXP-078 refraction cascade; 2026-04-13 Self-Contradicted→Std restored on curmudgeon gate-2 analysis that eclipse Sq tracks apparent not physical sun altitude). Both flips are well-reasoned individually, but the JSON now shows instability that a defender or auditor could surface ('the review team could not decide what verdict to apply within 24 hours'). The current verdict is correct — the flip record is what creates the optics issue. Not visible on the published site; relevant only for people reading wins.json or verdict_history directly (tinker/auditors).
holes_found.recommendation: No immediate change needed — the flip sequence is accurate and auditable. Optionally add a brief verdict_history annotation field explaining 'flip resolved on the apparent-vs-physical-shadow distinction; stable since 2026-04-13' so auditors do not have to reconstruct the reasoning. Low priority.

stronger_arguments.description: Sharpen the 'dome-vs-dome' nature of the Pearson r=0.9999 concession. Current verdict text says '(Pearson r = 0.9999 between predicted and claimed)' which is technically correct ('predicted' = us computing H(r)/r from the dome's V12 coordinate equation; 'claimed' = the dome's nine published FSF values). But 'predicted' is the same word the dome uses for prediction-vs-observation matches. Replace with explicit 'internal consistency (dome-formula FSF vs dome-published FSF)' language so a defender cannot quote-mine 'Pearson r=0.9999 agreement' as if it were agreement with nature. This also tightens hole #1's scope-leak risk.

Explicit 'any H(r) would produce this shape' disclaimer as a footnote rather than a main-text claim. Current P3 argues: 'The scaling shape is also not uniquely dome-geometric: any smooth monotonic H(r) combined with r·tan(θ) = H(r) produces a latitude-dependent ratio of the same form.' This is correct but invites a defender response: 'our H(r) was fixed in advance — the 0.1% match IS discriminating precision.' Mitigate by noting that a precision match between a pre-specified H(r) and a published FSF list is a MATHEMATICAL CONSISTENCY check, not an EMPIRICAL one — the published FSF values are computed from H(r) in the dome's own pipeline, so the agreement is expected and non-empirical.

Cite Yamazaki & Maute (2021) as the canonical modern Sq treatment more prominently. They are currently cited once (detail_evidence ¶3) as a URL but the key point — that modern Sq conductivity parametrizations derive station-specific eclipse magnitudes from INDEPENDENT data sources (ionosonde electron density, GPS/TEC, climatological averages) — is buried. This is the strongest non-discriminating argument: the standard model's inputs are eclipse-independent, while the dome's inputs are not.
sources: Yamazaki & Maute (2021), Frontiers in Astronomy and Space Sciences 8:766327

kernel_of_truth.description: The dome correctly identifies a real empirical pattern — Kim & Chang's 39-eclipse ensemble is genuine peer-reviewed science with a reproducible statistical signature (Y-component increase, X/Z/F decreases, magnetic-latitude 30°–50° peak, ~180-minute window). It also correctly notes that this magnetic response TRACKS SHADOW GEOMETRY and is distinguishable from thermal and gravitational hypotheses. And — acknowledged in our own verdict — the dome's FSF = H(r)/r scaling is a legitimate piece of dome geometry that reproduces its nine published FSF values to 0.1% via direct recomputation. None of that is fabricated; those components are real.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Three distinct problems: (1) the 1991–2016 ensemble counted by WIN-068 predates the dome's adoption by 8 years, was published independently, and attaches no dome-derived numerical prediction — it fails the post-hoc test under the dome author's own timestamping definition; (2) the ionospheric Sq mechanism (Chapman-framework, modern treatment in Yamazaki & Maute 2021) derives the same pattern from inputs sourced independently of the eclipse signal — the dome's cavity-dynamics story adds no numerical content over Sq; (3) the FSF concession is dome-vs-dome consistency (pre-specified formula reproduces pre-published FSF values) and is not invoked for the 1991–2016 ensemble at all; it belongs in WIN-010's scope, not WIN-068's. The claim is counted because there is signal to point to, not because the dome predicted anything about that signal prospectively.

deeper_analysis:
The ISS-1470/1471/1472 queue push cleanly addressed the three minor findings the decider tagged from c11 H2/H3/H4. Patches verified live in wins.json at review time (obscuration coverage present in P2; invoke→cite confirmed; 'numerical' qualifier present in Finding). No patch regression.

The interesting carry-forward is c11 H1 — the FSF scope leak. It was classified moderate in c11 and was NOT in the queue push scope (the decider triaged it for a future EXP-style restructure rather than an in-place sed patch, which is defensible). The c11 H1 symptom was the 'Even granting that FSF = H(r)/r...' framing. That specific framing is gone from the current text. But the structural problem — FSF validation prose lives inside WIN-068's verdict, detached from WIN-010 where FSF actually enters — persists in the 'Credit where it is due on the scaling itself...' paragraph (P2) and the 'For completeness: the dome's FSF scaling factor...' paragraph (detail_evidence P4). Both paragraphs are technically correct and are carefully scoped by surrounding text ('FSF does not enter the 1991–2016 data at all'), but they leave WIN-068 with a visible concession that a defender will quote.

The ISS-1472 patch inadvertently expanded the Chapman-mis-specialization footprint. The old Finding said 'the 1991–2016 Kim & Chang ensemble evaluated here attaches no dome-derived prediction.' The new Finding says 'Chapman (1933) ionospheric mechanism + Kim & Chang (2018) predict the 39-eclipse ensemble signal magnitude and geometry. ... attaches no dome-derived numerical prediction.' The narrowing qualifier 'numerical' is correct and additive. But the patch author pulled 'Chapman (1933) ionospheric mechanism' from detail_evidence ¶3 or detail_verdict_text ¶1 — where c11 H5 had already flagged the phrase as mis-specialized — and put it in the Finding. The defect now lives in three fields instead of two. Not a severe hole (minor), but the unified fix is clean: rewrite all three Chapman attributions in a single coordinated patch and resolve ISS-1441 at the same time by picking a canonical citation format (recommend 'Chapman (1919) Phil. Trans. A 218:1–118').

Cross-WIN check: WIN-010 uses 'Chapman (1933, Terr. Mag. Atmos. Electr.)' (no 38:3 suffix), WIN-025 uses 'Chapman (1933)' (no journal), WIN-068 uses 'Chapman (1933, Terrestrial Magnetism and Atmospheric Electricity 38:3)' (ambiguous volume-issue). Three formats across the cluster. Confirmed by direct inspection of wins.json.

Advocate mode / dome-defender position: The strongest defender attack on the current WIN-068 text is (a) grab the P2 FSF concession, reframe as 'review concedes FSF is real dome geometry with Pearson r=0.9999 — a 10,000-fold precision match', and (b) argue the 39-eclipse ensemble is itself a prospective test of the Sq/cavity mechanism because the dome cited it before any POST-2018 eclipses confirmed the pattern (not actually true — Kim & Chang 2018 is the ensemble, post-2018 eclipses do not appear in it). The counter to (a) is the dome-vs-dome scoping that P3/P5/Finding make explicit; the counter to (b) is the post_hoc P7 'no pre-registration in a venue independent of the author' line, which is strong. Overall the WIN-068 verdict structure is durable; the defender wedges are minor.

Confidence raised modestly (0.8→0.85) because the three ISS-1470/1471/1472 patches resolved the specific c11 H2/H3/H4 critiques cleanly with no regression; the residuals are triaged and tractable.

citations_verified: Kim & Chang (2018) Advances in Space Research 61:2040–2054 — verified as real peer-reviewed paper; title and DOI consistent with prior cycles' checks
Yamazaki & Maute (2021) Frontiers in Astronomy and Space Sciences 8:766327 — URL is well-formed; Sq conductivity framework content consistent with paper's known scope
citations_failed: Chapman (1933) 'Terrestrial Magnetism and Atmospheric Electricity 38:3' — citation format ambiguous (vol:page vs vol:issue); more importantly, Chapman's eclipse-Sq work is generally attributed to his 1919 Phil. Trans. paper and subsequent authors (Rastogi 1962, Malin 1973), not the 1933 lunar-tide paper. Potential mis-specialization. Cross-WIN inconsistency (WIN-010/025/068 each use a different Chapman citation format). Flagged in ISS-1441 and hole #2/#4 above.
citations_unchecked: Chapman (1919) Phil. Trans. A 218:1–118 (proposed replacement, pending analyst verification)
Rastogi (1962), Malin (1973) eclipse-Sq application references (proposed additions, pending analyst verification)
